+ Biopunk: Polymerase Chain Reaction — Score: 75.5
Prompt:
Biopunk -- Polymerase Chain Reaction Write a chapter set in a near-future post-apocalyptic world where corporate genetic engineering was designed with built-in obsolescence—engineered DNA lasting only one generation to maximize profits before societal collapse. Follow a biohacker working with salvaged equipment to splice viable food crops for their settlement using rapidly degrading genetic material. As world building / window dressing, include details about jury-rigged lab equipment, and the physical toll (and unexpected upsides) of using untested genetic modifications as medicine. The plot of this chapter is centred around a black market contract. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
### **Biopunk — Polymerase Chain Reaction** The solar panel rig flickered again, casting erratic shadows across the lab’s cluttered interior. Jace cursed under his breath as the power dipped, the centrifuge whining in protest before sputtering back to life. He adjusted the jury-rigged voltage stabilizer—three salvaged capacitors and a tangle of scavenged wiring—before turning his attention back to the PCR thermal cycler. The machine was old, its casing yellowed and cracked, but it was still functional. Barely. The problem wasn’t just the power. It was the DNA itself. Before everything collapsed, corporate agriculture had engineered obsolescence into the genome of every food crop they sold. A single generation. That was all the plants were designed for. Seeds went sterile, proteins degraded, and entire harvests withered to dust if you weren’t buying fresh stock. But no one was selling anymore. The corporations had fallen with the cities, leaving only biohackers like Jace to reverse-engineer the damage. And that was why he was here, in this makeshift lab behind reinforced steel doors, running a high-risk splice job for a black market buyer. He tapped the side of the PCR machine, watching the temperature cycle between denaturation, annealing, and extension. The polymerase enzymes were holding, and so far, the DNA hadn’t degraded beyond repair. If he could amplify enough viable strands, he could graft them into a compatible plant genome and get something that would grow more than once. Something that a settlement could live on. The door buzzer crackled—an old intercom repurposed from a scavenged apartment complex. Jace pulled the mic close. “Who is it?” A pause. Then a voice, low and wary. “Rami.” Jace exhaled. He disengaged the locks manually—no point in trusting the automated system with the power this unstable—and slid the heavy door open. Rami stepped inside, shaking off the dust of the wasteland. His coat was lined with a thin layer of ash from the dead farmlands beyond the city ruins. He pulled back his hood, revealing sharp eyes and the telltale paleness of someone who spent too much time avoiding the sun. “You have it?” he asked. Jace gestured to the PCR machine. “Still cycling. Another twenty minutes.” Rami grunted and took a seat on an overturned crate, watching the lab’s dim, flickering light. He was one of the few reliable brokers left, dealing in genetic salvage from the corpse of the old world. “What’s the job?” Jace asked. Rami pulled a sealed vial from his coat. The liquid inside shimmered faintly, the bioengineered proteins reacting to the movement. “Golden rice,” Rami said. “A strain engineered before things went to hell—high-yield, vitamin-enriched, but locked to a single generation. The client wants it fixed.” Jace took the vial, rolling it between his fingers. “This is second-gen. It’s already degrading.” “That’s why they’re paying.” Jace sighed. The vitamin-enhanced crops had been a corporate miracle, designed to combat malnutrition—but only if you had the means to buy fresh seeds every season. Now, with the engineered kill-switches embedded in the genome, the rice wouldn’t propagate. He set the vial down and moved to a corner of the lab where a row of plants grew in hydroponic trays. The leaves had an unnatural hue—some tinged violet, others shimmering faintly under the UV lamps. These were his test subjects, spliced with scavenged DNA, their properties unstable at best. He ran a hand over his forearm absentmindedly. The scars there told stories of failed modifications—gene patches meant to repair radiation damage, hastily-assembled CRISPR treatments that worked until they didn’t. His right eye still flickered with occasional bioluminescence from a failed night-vision experiment. But some things had worked. His immune system was stronger than it had ever been. The settlement’s children no longer suffered from the wasting sickness that had taken so many in the early years. Still, every splice came with a cost. Jace turned back to Rami. “If I do this, I need more than just payment. I need materials. Better polymerase, stable primers—hell, even a decent power supply.” Rami nodded. “The client has resources. I’ll make sure you get what you need.” Jace exhaled, rubbing his temples. He could feel the fatigue creeping in—long exposure to unstable genetic material had its own side effects. He grabbed a nutrient pack from the table and tore it open, drinking the thick, algae-based slurry inside. It tasted like old seaweed, but it kept him going. The PCR machine beeped. Jace moved quickly, extracting the amplified DNA with practiced hands. He loaded it into the splicer, setting up the integration sequence with a strain of wild rice he had been nurturing in the hydroponics bay. If the graft took, and if the kill-switch could be bypassed, they might just have a renewable food source again. A long silence stretched between them as the machine worked. Finally, Rami spoke. “You ever think we’re just repeating their mistakes?” Jace didn’t answer immediately. He looked at the plants, at the flickering solar panels, at the cracked hands that had rebuilt this lab from scraps of a lost civilization. “Maybe,” he admitted. “But at least this time, we’re keeping the knowledge in the hands of the people who need it.” Rami nodded. The machine let out another beep. Jace checked the readout. The splice had taken. The rice would grow. For now.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This biopunk piece demonstrates solid technical competence in worldbuilding and genre execution. The author successfully creates a believable post-apocalyptic setting where corporate genetic engineering with built-in obsolescence has led to societal collapse. The scientific details feel authentic without being overly technical, and the jury-rigged laboratory equipment is convincingly described. The protagonist Jace is well-characterized through his actions and environment rather than exposition. His scars, modified eye, and casual handling of dangerous genetic material effectively convey his expertise and the physical toll of his work. The dialogue between Jace and Rami feels natural and serves multiple purposes - advancing the plot, revealing character, and providing worldbuilding details. The writing maintains a consistent tone throughout, balancing technical detail with human elements. The prose is clean and functional, avoiding purple prose while still creating vivid imagery of the makeshift laboratory and post-apocalyptic setting. The author effectively uses specific details like the flickering solar panels, yellowed PCR machine, and algae-based nutrient packs to ground the reader in this world. However, the piece suffers from some predictability in its plot structure and character interactions. The black market contract setup is fairly standard for the genre, and the philosophical exchange between Jace and Rami about repeating past mistakes feels somewhat obligatory. The ending, while thematically appropriate, lacks emotional punch or surprise. The pacing is well-controlled, building tension through the technical process while developing character relationships. The author successfully integrates the required elements - salvaged equipment, genetic degradation, physical modifications, and black market dealings - without making them feel forced or checklist-driven. The emotional complexity is moderate but present, particularly in Jace's conflicted relationship with the technology he uses and his awareness of the risks involved. The piece avoids overwrought drama while maintaining engagement through its detailed world and competent character work. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 16 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Emotionally Complex: 12 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 5 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 14 Overall Impression: 15
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 16.0, Nuanced Characters: 14.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 16.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15.0, Elegant Prose: 15.0, Emotionally Engaging: 13.0, Emotionally Complex: 12.0, Coherent: 17.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 5.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 6.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8.0, Amateurish: 5.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 5.0, Unearned Transformations: 3.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 14.0, Overall Impression: 15.0
+ Iteration 2 — Avg Score: 77.1
+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 68.4
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 7: The Antares Proposal** Arthur Neegan tugged at his collar for the fifth time since boarding the transfer vessel. The stiff fabric of the high-collared jacket chafed against his neck, a far cry from the breathable, dust-resistant work suits he was accustomed to in the mines of Mars. The uniform had been waiting for him in his quarters aboard the shuttle—a subtle but unmistakable indication that he was no longer in control of his own circumstances. The vessel itself was a sleek, angular construct of polished titanium alloy, its interior adorned with deep blue and gold—colours unmistakably associated with the Antares Diplomatic Corps. That name was a misnomer, of course. The ADC was less a body of negotiation and more a projection of Antares' growing influence in the system. Their presence on Mars had been limited thus far, but the discovery of Azra had changed everything. Arthur gazed out of the narrow viewport. The crimson curve of Mars was slipping away beneath him, replaced by the cold, distant stars. The ship was en route to an orbital station, one of Antares' many waypoints scattered throughout the colonies. He had been given no details beyond that. The men who had come to his homestead had been polite, even cordial, but there had been no mistaking the weight of their sidearms or the unspoken finality of their invitation. The door to his cabin slid open with a soft hiss. A tall man in a crisp ADC uniform stepped inside, his hands clasped neatly behind his back. His face was lean, sharp, and utterly unreadable. "Mr. Neegan," the man said with a practiced smile. "I trust your journey has been comfortable?" "As comfortable as it can be when you're abducted from your own home," Arthur replied dryly. The man chuckled—a precise, measured sound. "Abduction is such an unpleasant word. Let's call it a necessary relocation in the interest of... opportunity." Arthur folded his arms. "And what opportunity would that be?" The man gestured toward the corridor. "If you'd follow me, all will be made clear." Arthur hesitated, but there was little point in resisting. He followed the man through a series of narrow, polished corridors, the faint hum of the ship's artificial gravity systems thrumming beneath his boots. Eventually, they arrived at a small conference chamber. The far wall was a panoramic window, offering a breathtaking view of the stars beyond. Seated at the head of a sleek, metallic table was a woman dressed in the deep blue of the ADC. She was older, perhaps in her late fifties, with silver-streaked hair pulled into a severe knot. Her expression was calm, calculating. "Mr. Neegan," she greeted him. "Please, sit." Arthur took the offered chair, remaining stiff as the tall man took a position at her flank. "My name is Ambassador Liora Sareth," the woman said. "I represent certain interests within the Antares Directorate." Arthur exhaled. "And what does Antares want with me?" Sareth leaned forward slightly. "You are an engineer, Mr. Neegan. A man of practical knowledge. So I will not insult you with misdirection. We both know what lies beneath your claim on Mars. The Azra deposit is... substantial." Arthur kept his expression neutral. "I imagine plenty of corporations would be interested in that vein." "Indeed. And not just corporations." Sareth steepled her fingers. "Azra is a resource that will shape the future of interstellar power. The Sol Federation, the Belt Syndicates, the independent colonial blocs—each of them is vying for control. The question is not whether Azra will be exploited, but by whom." Arthur frowned. "And you assume I want to throw in with Antares?" Sareth smiled thinly. "I assume nothing. But I do know that without powerful allies, you will not be able to hold onto your claim. The moment word spreads, you will be beset on all sides—by competitors, by governments, by those who would rather see you disappear than negotiate." Arthur tapped his fingers against the table. "So this is a protection racket?" Sareth chuckled. "Not at all. This is an offer of partnership. Antares has the resources to ensure your safety, to help you develop your claim under our auspices. In return, we would expect a... preferential arrangement regarding Azra extraction and distribution." Arthur narrowed his gaze. "And if I refuse?" Sareth's smile never wavered. "Then I imagine your competitors will be rather keen to make their own offers. Some will be less diplomatic than we are." Arthur sat back, considering. The truth was, he had known from the moment he uncovered the Azra vein that his life would never be the same. He had hoped for time—to plan, to strategize—but that luxury was already gone. The only question now was which hand he would shake, and what price he would pay for it. He glanced toward the viewport again, the cold stars watching in silence. "I assume you have terms in mind?" Sareth nodded. "Naturally. Antares will provide security for your claim, legal and otherwise. We will ensure that your operations remain unimpeded. In exchange, we require exclusive first access to all Azra extracted under your name. You will be well-compensated, of course." Arthur exhaled slowly. "You make it sound like I have a choice." "You do," Sareth assured him. "You can walk away, return to Mars, and attempt to navigate this storm alone. But you will not last long. Not with what you have found." Arthur studied her, searching for any crack in her polished veneer. There was none. The Antares Diplomatic Corps lived up to its reputation—polite, professional, and utterly ruthless. Finally, he nodded, just once. "I'll need time to consider." Sareth inclined her head. "Of course. But do not take too long, Mr. Neegan. The universe moves swiftly, and patience is a luxury that neither of us can afford." She gestured to the tall man, who stepped forward. "Captain Varin will see you back to your quarters. We will speak again soon." Arthur rose, his mind already racing through possibilities, contingencies. He had expected to be caught in a struggle over his claim. He had not expected the struggle to begin so soon. As he followed Captain Varin back through the corridors, Arthur finally stopped adjusting his collar. The discomfort of the fabric was nothing compared to the weight settling on his shoulders.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a solid understanding of classic science fiction conventions and successfully captures many elements of Asimov's style. The author establishes a clear political intrigue scenario with corporate interests vying for control of a valuable resource, which aligns well with Asimov's tendency to explore large-scale societal implications through individual characters. The world-building is competent, establishing the Antares Diplomatic Corps as a thinly-veiled corporate military force and positioning Arthur within a larger galactic power struggle. The retrofuturistic elements are present but subtle - polished titanium alloys, artificial gravity systems, and interstellar politics create the appropriate atmosphere. The dialogue serves its functional purpose, moving the plot forward and establishing the stakes. Ambassador Sareth's measured, diplomatic language contrasts effectively with Arthur's more direct, working-class responses. However, the exchanges feel somewhat mechanical and lack the philosophical depth that characterizes Asimov's best work. The conversation follows predictable beats without surprising insights or memorable turns of phrase. Arthur's characterization remains consistent with his established role as a practical engineer, but he lacks psychological complexity. His reactions are reasonable but not particularly revealing of deeper character traits or internal conflicts. Sareth is competently portrayed as a polished corporate operative, though she doesn't transcend the archetype. The pacing is appropriate, building tension through the journey to the meeting and the gradual revelation of Antares' proposal. The ending appropriately leaves Arthur (and readers) in suspense about his ultimate decision. However, the prose occasionally relies on telling rather than showing, particularly in exposition about the political situation. Some descriptions feel perfunctory rather than vivid. The piece lacks the intellectual rigor and philosophical questioning that elevates Asimov's work above mere space opera. The stakes feel appropriately large-scale, but the human element could be more compelling. The writing is technically proficient but doesn't achieve the elegant simplicity that characterizes the best classic science fiction. It reads more like competent genre fiction than a work that captures Asimov's distinctive voice and thematic concerns. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 16 Believable Character Actions: 15 Nuanced Characters: 12 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 14 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 13 Elegant Prose: 12 Emotionally Engaging: 11 Emotionally Complex: 10 Coherent: 16 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 9 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 10 Amateurish: 7 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Sentences Flow Naturally: 14 Overall Reader Engagement: 12 Overall Impression: 13

+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 81.6
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Darkroom Revelations** The darkroom hums with the low whir of the ventilation fan, the air thick with the scent of chemicals. Under the red light, Elliot’s profile is a study in contrast—sharp cheekbones, the curve of his lips softened by the glow. He’s watching our print emerge in the developer tray, his fingers hovering just above the surface of the liquid, as if afraid to disturb it. I should be watching, too. Instead, I’m watching him. I lean against the counter, arms crossed, pretending I’m just surveying our work. “You always get this focused when you print?” Elliot glances up, his eyes catching the light just right—dark, but reflective. “I like the process,” he says. “The way an image appears out of nothing. It feels... I don’t know. More real than digital.” His voice is soft but certain, like he’s thought about this a lot and only now decided to say it out loud. I nod, though my mind is still half-stuck on the way he’s standing—his shoulders hunched slightly inward, like he’s trying to take up less space. I’ve always noticed how he moves, how he dresses. The thrift-shop nu-goth aesthetic, the way he layers oversized sweaters over lace-trimmed shirts, how his rings clink softly when he fidgets. I’ve never been into guys like that. Hell, I’ve never really been into guys at all. But Elliot has always been something else. Something in between. My phone buzzes in my pocket. I ignore it. He exhales, barely audible above the hum of the room. “I’ve been thinking about something,” he says, still watching the print shift from shadow to clarity. I don’t push. I’ve learned that with Elliot, silence is space. He’ll step into it when he’s ready. After a beat, he glances at me. “I don’t think I’m... just a guy.” The words hang between us, delicate and sharp. I stay still, careful. “Yeah?” Elliot presses his lips together, then nods. “I mean. I don’t know exactly what I am. But I know I don’t feel right in the box I was given.” He exhales a quiet laugh, shaking his head. “I don’t even know why I’m telling you this.” I uncross my arms, gripping the edge of the counter instead. “Maybe because you trust me.” His gaze flickers to mine, searching. “Do you think that’s stupid?” I huff a laugh. “You’ve seen me handle caustic chemicals on no sleep. You tell me.” That earns me a small smile, fleeting but real. “I guess I just... I feel like you see things other people don’t. Like you don’t expect me to be something I’m not.” I swallow. Hard. Because he’s right. And because I suddenly don’t know what to do with the heat curling in my chest. I’ve always been the one in control. In art, in relationships, in how I present myself to the world. I like structure. Sharp lines. Bold choices. Elliot is none of those things. He’s shifting and soft-edged and undefined. And yet, I can’t stop looking at him. “What does that mean for you?” I ask, keeping my voice even. He shrugs, but it’s not casual. “I don’t know yet. I just know that dressing the way I do feels right. And sometimes I think about using different pronouns, or a different name, but it feels... big. Scary.” He hesitates, then adds, “But also exciting.” I nod slowly. “Yeah. I get that.” And I do. More than I thought I would. Elliot looks down at the print, at his own hands reflected in the glossy surface. “Would it be weird if I said I was kind of relieved you’re the one here when I’m saying this out loud?” Something in my chest tightens. “Not weird. Just good timing.” His lips twitch like he wants to smile but isn’t sure if he should. “Maybe.” The silence stretches again, but it’s not uncomfortable. Just charged. Like there’s something else waiting to be said, but neither of us knows how to start it. I clear my throat, reaching for the print tongs. “So, do you want me to start calling you something different?” Elliot’s fingers tap against the counter. “Not yet. But maybe.” A pause. “Would you? If I asked?” I meet his gaze, steady. “Yeah. Of course.” Something in his face softens. And then something shifts. The air between us changes, like the moment in the darkroom when an image finally comes into focus. I can feel the weight of his attention on me now, the way his fingers twitch like he wants to reach for something but isn’t sure if he’s allowed. I’ve always been the one to make the first move. But tonight, I don’t want to take anything from him. I want to let him decide. So I wait. Elliot hesitates, then steps closer, close enough that I can see the way his breath moves his shirt, the silver glint of his rings. “Dani.” My name, low and careful and something else I can’t name. “Yeah?” He swallows. “Does this change things for you?” It’s the question I’ve been avoiding all semester, the one I don’t have an answer for. I don’t know what to do with the way I feel about him, about this version of him that’s still unfolding. I don’t know what it means for me, for what I’ve always thought I wanted. But I do know one thing. I lift a hand, slow enough that he has time to move away if he wants to. He doesn’t. When my fingers brush his wrist, his pulse jumps. “No,” I say. “It just makes them clearer.” Elliot exhales, and it’s like I can feel it against my skin. He tilts his head slightly, searching my face, and I let him. Let him take the lead, for once. The darkroom hums around us, the red light casting everything in shades of almost. But this—this moment—feels real. Like the first sharp edge of an image appearing on paper. And I can’t wait to see what develops next.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional intelligence in handling a delicate subject matter. The narrative successfully captures Dani's perspective as requested, maintaining her assertive personality while showing vulnerability and growth. The darkroom setting is effectively utilized both literally and metaphorically, creating an intimate confessional atmosphere that serves the story well. The characterization is particularly strong. Dani's voice feels authentic - her usual take-charge attitude is present but tempered by genuine care and uncertainty. The internal conflict between wanting to protect Elliot while respecting their agency is well-rendered. Elliot emerges as a fully realized character despite being seen through Dani's eyes, with believable hesitation and courage in their self-revelation. The dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, avoiding both overly clinical language and unrealistic eloquence. The conversations have the right amount of awkwardness and tentative exploration that the situation demands. The pacing allows for meaningful silences and builds tension effectively. The prose is generally elegant without being overwrought. The sensory details of the darkroom - the chemical smells, red light, ventilation hum - ground the scene effectively. The photography metaphors (images emerging from nothing, coming into focus) work well without being heavy-handed. The emotional complexity is handled with nuance. Dani's confusion about her own identity and attraction is portrayed realistically, as is her excitement at witnessing Elliot's self-discovery. The piece avoids easy answers while still providing emotional satisfaction. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose edges toward being slightly precious, and the ending, while emotionally satisfying, resolves perhaps a bit too neatly given the complexity of the situation. However, these are minor issues in an otherwise well-crafted piece that successfully navigates challenging subject matter with sensitivity and skill. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 6 Unearned Transformations: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Contemporary romance / drama: Stolen Moments in Roma — Score: 72.2
Prompt:
Contemporary Romance / Drama: Stolen Moments in Roma. Write a series of interconnected short scenes and dialogues that span a single day in modern-day Rome, in narrative form. This will form the beginning of a larger story. Alessandra, a fiery, independent Roman woman who works as a motorcycle courier, nearly collides with Ben, a somewhat clumsy, overly polite British academic on sabbatical, who is engrossed in a guidebook near the Trevi Fountain. The initial encounter is a clash of cultures and personalities – Alessandra is all sharp wit and rapid-fire Italian, while Ben is flustered apologies and awkward attempts at speaking the language. However, as they sort out the near-miss, a spark of unexpected humor and connection emerges. Alessandra, it quickly becomes clear, is a whirlwind of energy and mischief. She's a self-admitted troublemaker with a secret penchant for kleptomania, a detail she doesn't explicitly reveal, but which manifests in subtle, daring acts throughout the day. She impulsively decides to take Ben on a wild, unsanctioned tour of Rome, dragging him in some specific instances far outside his comfort zone. Ben, despite his initial apprehension and inherent politeness, finds himself utterly captivated by Alessandra's unapologetic spirit and zest for life. He is drawn into her orbit, a willing participant in increasingly outlandish situations, a quiet rebellion brewing beneath his scholarly exterior. The narrative should primarily consist of short, vivid scenes and dialogue exchanges, revealing their contrasting personalities and backgrounds, and the growing, ambiguous tension between them. Ben is captivated by Alessandra's energy and the thrill of breaking free from his routine. There's a clear sense that Alessandra is toying with Ben, pushing his boundaries and enjoying his reactions, but her own feelings remain somewhat enigmatic. The day should be a rollercoaster of exhilarating, slightly dangerous, and humorous moments, leaving Ben completely disoriented but utterly smitten. Explore the underlying tension & chemistry between them, culminating in some payoff as they part ways (or not -- your choice). Include this detail: Write in narrative prose, using immersive storytelling. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Stolen Moments in Roma** --- ### **Scene 1: A Near Collision at the Trevi Fountain** Ben was engrossed in his well-worn guidebook, squinting at the tiny map section, when the world erupted around him. A blur of red—Alessandra’s motorcycle—came skidding to a halt inches from his knees. The screech of brakes. A sharp inhale from the tourists around them. Ben stumbled back, his guidebook slipping from his hands. “Cazzo!” Alessandra snapped, yanking off her helmet, a cascade of dark curls spilling free. “Sei cieco? Vuoi morire oggi?” Ben blinked at her, wide-eyed and stunned. His Italian wasn’t great, but he caught the gist. “I—I’m terribly sorry,” he stammered, his voice thick with his London accent. “I didn’t see—” “No, you didn’t,” she cut in, swinging a leg off her bike. Her boots hit the cobblestones with a decisive thud. “Tourists. Always walking around like lost sheep.” Ben opened his mouth, then shut it again. He had, in fact, been acting exactly like a lost sheep. But it wasn’t his fault the streets of Rome twisted in a way that defied all logic. “I was just trying to find—” “Let me guess,” Alessandra interrupted, rolling her eyes. “Fontana di Trevi?” Ben glanced over his shoulder at the glistening fountain. “Er… yes. That’s where I am, isn’t it?” Alessandra let out a sharp laugh, tilting her head. “You are hopeless, professore.” Ben flushed. “I’m not a professor. I’m on sabbatical.” “Same thing.” She smirked, then bent down, scooping up his guidebook. As she handed it back, her fingers brushed his. “You know, this book is useless.” Ben frowned. “I find it quite—” “Boring,” she declared. “If you want to see the real Rome, you need me.” Ben hesitated. There was something about the glint in her eyes—mischief, fire, a challenge. He knew, instinctively, that Alessandra was the kind of person who made sensible men do very stupid things. And yet— “Alright,” he said, surprising himself. “Show me.” --- ### **Scene 2: The Detour** Alessandra didn’t give him time to reconsider. She grabbed his wrist and pulled him through the maze of narrow streets, dodging tourists and weaving past market stalls with an ease that left Ben breathless. “Where are we going?” he asked, stumbling slightly as she yanked him down a side alley. “No questions,” she said. “Just keep up.” They emerged into a quieter piazza, where an elderly vendor was selling roasted chestnuts. Alessandra flashed the man a dazzling smile, then, with a flick of her wrist, swiped a single chestnut from his cart as she passed. Ben saw it. He wasn’t sure if he was supposed to. “You didn’t—” “Shh.” She winked and tossed him the stolen prize. “Eat.” Ben hesitated, then cracked it open. It was warm, smoky, delicious. He chewed thoughtfully. “You do that often?” Alessandra shrugged, utterly unapologetic. “I take what I want.” Ben wasn’t sure if she meant the chestnut or something else entirely. --- ### **Scene 3: A Daring Escape** Somehow, they ended up in the back corridors of a centuries-old palazzo. “This isn’t legal, is it?” Ben whispered as Alessandra led him through a dimly lit hallway. “Not exactly,” she admitted, grinning. “Alessandra—” “Shh! We are making history, professore.” Ben swallowed his objections as she pushed open a heavy wooden door. Suddenly, they were standing on a hidden rooftop terrace overlooking the Pantheon. The city sprawled below them, golden in the afternoon light. Ben exhaled. “It’s… astonishing.” Alessandra leaned against the railing, watching him instead of the view. “You spend too much time inside your head,” she observed. Ben turned to her. “And you don’t spend enough time thinking things through.” She laughed. “Maybe.” A beat of silence. Then, impulsively, she plucked his guidebook from his hands and tossed it over the edge. Ben let out a strangled sound. “That was my—” “Now you’re free,” she declared. Ben stared at her, half in disbelief, half in something else entirely. --- ### **Scene 4: The Downpour** The first raindrop hit Ben’s nose. Then another. Within seconds, the sky cracked open. Alessandra grabbed his hand, pulling him into a sprint. They darted through the streets, dodging café awnings and laughing as the rain soaked them through. Finally, Alessandra yanked him under the awning of a gelato shop. They stood there, breathless, dripping, the scent of sugar and cream filling the air. Ben pushed his wet hair from his face. “You planned this, didn’t you?” Alessandra grinned. “I control the weather now?” Ben chuckled, shaking his head. “You’re impossible.” She stepped closer, her dark eyes flickering with something unreadable. “And yet, you’re still here.” Ben swallowed. “Yes.” A long pause. The sound of rain drumming against the awning. Then, Alessandra reached past him, swiping something from the counter inside. Ben blinked. “Did you just steal a spoon?” She popped it between her lips, smirking. “Maybe.” Ben exhaled, a mix of exasperation and fascination. “You really don’t play by the rules, do you?” Alessandra tilted her head. “And you do?” Ben hesitated. Then, surprising even himself, he reached over and plucked a gelato cup from the counter. He held it up in mock challenge. Alessandra’s grin widened. “Benvenuto nel lato oscuro, professore.” --- ### **Scene 5: The Goodbye (Or Not?)** The rain had stopped by the time they reached the bridge overlooking the Tiber. The sky was turning indigo, the city lights flickering to life. Ben knew this was the moment. The point where the whirlwind ended, where he returned to who he was before today. “I should…” He trailed off. Alessandra watched him, something unreadable in her expression. “You should.” Neither of them moved. Finally, she reached into her pocket and pressed something into his hand. Ben looked down. His wallet. He hadn’t even noticed it was missing. His gaze snapped back to hers, startled. Alessandra smirked. “You need to be more careful.” Ben laughed, shaking his head. “You’re unbelievable.” She winked. “I know.” A beat. Then, impulsively, Ben did the most reckless thing he’d done all day. He leaned in and kissed her. It was brief, unexpected—both of them breathless as they pulled away. Alessandra studied him for a moment. Then, without a word, she turned and disappeared into the Roman night. Ben stood there, heart pounding, utterly lost. And for the first time in his life, he didn’t mind at all. --- **To be continued…**
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates solid competence in contemporary romance writing with clear strengths in character differentiation and pacing. The author successfully establishes the contrasting personalities of Alessandra and Ben through their actions and dialogue - she's impulsive and rule-breaking, he's cautious and proper. The kleptomania detail is woven throughout naturally, from the chestnut theft to the wallet reveal, showing rather than telling this aspect of her character. The structure works well, with five distinct scenes that build momentum and intimacy. The progression from near-collision to kiss feels earned within the compressed timeframe. The dialogue captures the cultural clash effectively, with Alessandra's sharp directness contrasting Ben's apologetic politeness. The Italian phrases add authenticity without overwhelming non-Italian speakers. However, the writing suffers from some predictable romance tropes. The "proper academic meets wild local" setup is well-worn territory, and while executed competently, it doesn't offer fresh insights. Some moments feel slightly forced - the guidebook tossing scene borders on contrived, and Ben's sudden transformation from cautious to gelato-stealing feels rushed. The prose is generally clean and readable, though it occasionally lapses into telling rather than showing emotions. Phrases like "something unreadable in her expression" appear multiple times, becoming a crutch. The ending kiss, while satisfying for the genre, arrives somewhat predictably. The piece succeeds in its primary goal of establishing chemistry and setting up a larger story. The Roman setting is evoked effectively through specific locations and sensory details. The rain scene provides good atmospheric tension, and the wallet revelation is a clever callback that recontextualizes earlier interactions. The writing demonstrates technical competence with natural sentence flow and consistent pacing. While not groundbreaking, it delivers what the prompt requested with professional execution and genuine charm. + Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 69.8
Prompt:
Cozy spacepunk The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter Seven: A Stranger's Request** The Last Outpost hums with the usual lull of conversation, punctuated by the occasional burst of laughter or the clatter of glasses meeting the bar. The station's artificial gravity is acting up again—nothing dramatic, just a soft, occasional lurch that makes the drinks ripple. I brace myself against the counter out of habit, flipping a bar rag over my shoulder as I pour a round of drinks for the table in the corner. Then the stranger slides onto a stool in front of me. "Vega whisky, neat," they say, voice low, tight. "Better make that a double." I glance up from the bottle in my hand. Their tentacled dreads are askew, like they’ve been running—or worse, hiding. Their shoulders are tight, their breathing a hair too fast for someone just here to unwind. I pour the drink, sliding it across the bar. "Rough night?" They snort softly, shaking their head. "Something like that." They lift the glass, down half of it in one go, then exhale. "You run this place?" I laugh. "I wish. No, just the one keeping everyone’s drinks full and their problems manageable." I gesture around vaguely. "The Outpost runs itself, more or less." "Convenient," they murmur. They roll the glass between their fingers, watching the amber liquid catch the dim bar lights. I lean forward, lowering my voice. "Look, I don’t know what’s got you wound tighter than a broken airlock, but if trouble’s about to follow you in here, I’d appreciate a little warning." The stranger meets my gaze, studying me. Their eyes flicker with something—uncertainty? Guilt? I can’t tell, but I know that look. I’ve worn it myself. "You get a lot of trouble in here?" they ask. "Depends on your definition," I say. "Miners get rowdy, merchants get cheated, hearts get broken. The usual." I tilt my head. "You planning on adding to the mix?" They hesitate, then set their glass down carefully. "I just need somewhere to lay low for a while. A few hours, maybe." I wipe down the counter, thinking. "That why you came to a hole-in-the-wall waystation on the edge of nowhere?" "Not exactly a tourist destination," they admit. "But it’s quiet. And quiet is good right now." "Quiet is relative," I say, nodding toward the far side of the bar, where two off-duty haulers are getting into a heated argument over a game of grav-dice. "You got a name?" They hesitate again, then say, "Call me Reth." The name means nothing to me. That doesn’t mean they’re not trouble. Before I can ask anything else, the gravity flickers again—just enough to make the bottles behind the bar shift against each other with a soft clink. It’s worse than usual tonight. I make a mental note to mention it to Val, our part-time engineer, when she rolls in later. Reth doesn’t seem to notice the fluctuation. They’re too busy watching the entrance. I follow their gaze. The doors remain shut. "You expecting someone?" I ask. Reth exhales, rubbing a hand over their face. "Hoping I lost them before I got here." That gets my attention. I lower my voice even more. "Who’s them?" They don’t answer right away. Instead, they finish their drink in one long pull, then push the glass toward me. "Another." I pour, but I don’t let the conversation drop. "You running from someone, Reth?" They look at me again, this time with something like calculation. "Hypothetically?" I raise an eyebrow. They sigh. "Fine. Yes. I didn’t exactly part ways amicably with my last employer." "Ah," I say. "Employer trouble. That I understand." "Yeah?" I tap the bar top with my fingers. "Let’s just say you’re not the only one around here who’s had to change career paths." Reth’s lips twitch like they might smile, but then they glance toward the entrance again. Their fingers tighten around their glass. I don’t like it. Not one bit. "Listen," I say. "If you’ve got people looking for you, the Outpost isn’t exactly a fortress. You planning on staying long enough to make things interesting for the rest of us?" Reth shakes their head. "I don’t want trouble for anyone else. Just need a place to breathe. That’s all." I study them for a moment. They’re tense, but not reckless. Desperate, but not stupid. And something about them—it’s familiar. Not their face, not their voice, but the way they hold themselves. Like someone backed into a corner, trying to keep their balance. I recognize it because I’ve been there before. "Alright," I say finally. "You get one drink’s worth of sanctuary. After that, if whoever’s looking for you comes through that door, you deal with it outside." Reth nods, relief flickering across their face. "Fair enough." But as I turn away to serve another customer, the bar doors hiss open, and I hear the shift in the room’s energy before I even look. Conversation dips, not entirely stopping, but slowing, stuttering. A few glances flick toward the entrance and then quickly back to their drinks. Whoever just walked in, they’re not regulars. I glance back at Reth. Their fingers are white-knuckled around their glass. I don’t need them to tell me that their past just walked through my door.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates solid competence in establishing a spacepunk bar setting with appropriate atmosphere and tension. The author successfully creates the character of Nets as a bartender with a mysterious past, using first-person present tense as requested. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes - revealing character, advancing plot, and building tension. The writing establishes conflict effectively through the arrival of Reth, a clearly troubled stranger seeking refuge, followed by the ominous entrance of unnamed pursuers at chapter's end. The pacing builds appropriately, and the author wisely avoids resolving the central conflict within this chapter as instructed. However, the piece suffers from several notable weaknesses. The worldbuilding feels generic - "tentacled dreads" and "Vega whisky" are surface-level sci-fi elements that don't create a truly distinctive universe. The characterization, while competent, relies heavily on familiar archetypes: the bartender with a past, the desperate fugitive, the mysterious pursuers. There's little that surprises or subverts expectations. The prose is workmanlike but lacks distinctive voice or memorable imagery. Descriptions like "The Last Outpost hums with the usual lull of conversation" are functional but uninspired. The artificial gravity malfunction feels like set dressing rather than meaningful worldbuilding. The dialogue percentage appears to meet the 60% requirement, and conversations feel realistic, but they don't reveal particularly deep character insights. Nets and Reth's exchange follows predictable patterns of cautious probing and reluctant revelation. The ending creates appropriate tension but feels somewhat mechanical - the timing of the pursuers' arrival is convenient rather than organic. The piece reads like competent genre fiction but lacks the spark that would elevate it beyond workmanlike execution. + Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 76.6
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Gone South – Chapter Two** The guy who gave me a ride said he was “heading into the city” like that meant something, like the city was just one place instead of a tangle of streets and people and history I didn’t know. I didn’t ask questions. I just nodded and stared out the window as we crossed some huge-ass bridge, the water below dark and endless. Then, just as suddenly as he’d picked me up at a gas station in Alabama, he was kicking me out at some intersection in New Orleans. And now I’m here. The pavement is still warm from last night, the air thick and sticky even though the sun’s barely up. I don’t know where I am, exactly. French Quarter, maybe? I’ve seen it in movies. The buildings are old, with those curling iron balconies, and the streets are narrow, littered with last night's trash—beads, cigarette butts, a crumpled flyer for a psychic. I check my phone. 12% battery. No charger. No plan. I lean against a brick wall, breathing in deep, trying not to panic. I did this. I left. I made it out. That’s the important part. My chest tightens anyway. I need a place to think. A place to figure out what the hell comes next. ### Café smells pull me down the street—coffee, sugar, something fried. My stomach knots, empty and pissed off at me. I still have twenty bucks left from the gas station job I worked for two weeks before quitting. Not much, but enough for breakfast. The café is open-air, the kind of place where people sit for hours, sipping tiny cups of espresso, pretending they have nowhere better to be. A waitress eyes me as I walk in—my hoodie is wrinkled, my jeans stiff with yesterday’s sweat—but she doesn’t stop me. I grab a table in the corner, away from the tourists. I’m halfway through a plate of beignets when someone slides into the chair across from me. "You look like shit," the stranger says. I blink at them, mid-chew. Powdered sugar dusts their brown fingers, their chipped black nail polish flashing as they steal one of my beignets without asking. They’re my age, maybe a little older. Dark curls shoved under a ragged beanie, sharp cheekbones, eyes that don’t miss a thing. "Thanks," I say after swallowing. "Real nice of you." They smirk. "New in town?" I shrug. "Something like that." "Figured. You’ve got that ‘what the fuck am I doing here?’ look." They take another bite, licking sugar from their thumb. "Name’s Jules." I hesitate. I don’t know this person. I don’t know *anyone* here. But what am I gonna do, lie? Make up a name? "Taylor," I say. Jules nods, like that answers something. "So, Taylor. You running from something, or to something?" The question punches me in the ribs. I don’t answer. Jules doesn’t push. They just lean back in their chair, stretching like a cat. "You got a place to stay?" I shake my head. "Figured," they say again. "Come on." They’re already up, already moving, not waiting to see if I’ll follow. I hesitate. I could stay. Finish my food. Try to figure shit out on my own. But then I look at my phone again. 10% battery. No charger. I grab my plate, shove the last beignet in my mouth, and follow Jules into the city. ### Jules leads me through twisting streets, past bars still clinging to the last dredges of last night’s chaos. We cross into a quieter part of the city, where the sidewalks are cracked and the houses lean too close together. Finally, they stop in front of a faded turquoise house with peeling paint and a porch sagging under its own weight. Inside, it smells like incense and old books. A fan creaks overhead, barely stirring the thick air. "You live here?" I ask. "For now," Jules says, kicking off their boots. "It’s a squat. No one’s lived here for years, but the water still runs, and the roof mostly holds. Good enough." I stand awkwardly in the doorway, unsure if I should step inside. Jules flops onto a ripped-up couch, patting the spot next to them. I sit. "You got a plan?" they ask. I laugh, sharp and humorless. "Nope." Jules studies me, their gaze heavy. "Yeah. Me neither." I don’t ask what they’re running from. They don’t ask me again, either. Maybe it doesn’t matter. Maybe we’re just two kids who ended up in the same place, lost in the same way. Jules tosses me a charger. "Don’t say I never did anything for you." I plug in my phone. 9% battery. The screen lights up with missed messages. I don’t check them. Not yet. Jules pulls a crumpled cigarette from their pocket, flicks a lighter. "Welcome to New Orleans," they say. I exhale. For the first time in two days, I don’t feel like I’m about to drown.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates solid competence in young adult fiction writing with several notable strengths and some areas for improvement. The author successfully captures Taylor's voice as a transgender teen runaway, maintaining consistency in their cynical yet vulnerable perspective throughout. The first-person narration feels authentic to the character's age and circumstances, with natural-sounding internal monologue that effectively conveys their emotional state without becoming overwrought. The setting of New Orleans is well-established through sensory details - the warm pavement, thick air, coffee and sugar smells, and the visual elements of the French Quarter. The author avoids heavy-handed description while still creating a vivid sense of place. The introduction of Jules as another runaway creates immediate intrigue and provides Taylor with a potential ally whose motivations remain mysterious. The dialogue between Taylor and Jules feels natural and age-appropriate, with Jules's direct, somewhat abrasive personality coming through clearly. Their interaction develops organically from suspicion to tentative trust. The pacing moves well from Taylor's initial disorientation to finding temporary refuge. However, the piece has some limitations. While competently executed, the scenario of runaway teens finding each other and temporary shelter follows fairly predictable patterns. Jules's character, while engaging, relies somewhat on familiar archetypes of the street-smart runaway. The emotional complexity, while present, could be deeper - Taylor's processing of their family rejection and gender identity struggles feels somewhat surface-level given the magnitude of these experiences. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - not overly optimistic but offering a moment of relief that feels earned after Taylor's difficult journey. The transformation from desperation to cautious hope is gradual and believable. The prose flows naturally without pretension, though it occasionally lacks distinctive flair. Overall, this is solid, engaging young adult fiction that fulfills its premise competently while leaving room for greater emotional depth and originality. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 15 Overall Impression: 15

+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 77.0
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
### **My Apocalypse Buddy** The morning started, as it often did, with a debate about breakfast. "I think we should eat the last can of beans," Midas said, staring at the dented tin as if sheer force of will could make it more appetizing. Chewy, the animo-tronic stuffed koala, sat across from him, his fabric-covered servos whirring slightly. His LED eyes flickered red, which meant disapproval. Or possibly indigestion. It was sometimes hard to tell. "If we eat the beans now," Midas continued, rubbing the stubble on his chin, "we'll have nothing left for tomorrow. But if we go out scavenging today, we might find more food." Chewy’s eyes flickered yellow—skepticism. "Look, I know yesterday didn’t go so well." Chewy tilted his head, and his eyes flashed a brief but emphatic purple. Yesterday had involved a pack of feral dogs, an unexpectedly explosive propane tank, and a very short-lived attempt at diplomacy with a man named Gristle. "But today," Midas declared, "today will be different." Chewy’s eyes turned blue, which Midas took as reluctant agreement. Or possibly resignation. ### **The Mission** Their destination was a supermarket on the far side of town. Allegedly untouched. Allegedly safe. Allegedly full of supplies. Midas knew better than to trust allegations, but hunger had a way of making a man optimistic. Their first obstacle was the bridge. Or rather, the lack of it. "Pretty sure this was here last time," Midas muttered. The concrete supports were still standing, but the middle section had vanished, replaced by a spectacular drop into a river that had seen better days. Chewy’s eyes flashed green—amusement. "Not helping." They considered their options. Going around would take hours. Swimming was out, since Midas had no interest in growing an extra limb from whatever was in that water. "Guess we climb down and back up," Midas said, already regretting it. ### **The Descent** Midas had never been particularly good at climbing. Or balance. Or any activity that required a combination of muscles and foresight. Things went well for about four feet. Then a rock betrayed him, and he tumbled the rest of the way in a series of undignified flails, landing in a pile of suspiciously soft trash. Chewy, who had wisely waited to be carried, landed gracefully beside him. His eyes blinked pink—concern. "I'm fine," Midas groaned. "Nothing broken. Except my pride. And possibly my spine." They scrambled up the far side with considerably more caution (and significantly more muttered curses). ### **The Supermarket** The supermarket was still standing, which was a good start. The front doors had been barricaded, but someone had helpfully left a hole in the side wall, presumably for convenience or crime. "See? Easy," Midas whispered as they slipped inside. Then he stepped on a rat. The rat, understandably upset, let out a shriek and bolted. Midas, also understandably upset, let out a slightly less dignified shriek and nearly knocked over a shelf. Somewhere deeper in the store, something growled. Chewy’s eyes turned red. "Right. Time to go." They grabbed what they could—some cans, a suspiciously intact loaf of bread, a bottle of something that might have once been cola—and sprinted for the exit. Something large and previously asleep was now very awake and very interested in them. "NOT TODAY," Midas shouted, hurling the loaf of bread in an act of misguided self-defense. The creature—a mutated bear, or possibly just a very angry raccoon—was unimpressed. Chewy, in a rare moment of initiative, lunged forward and bit the creature’s ear. Or at least, that’s what Midas assumed. In reality, Chewy’s tiny servos whirred as he latched onto the beast’s fur, his little plush teeth doing absolutely nothing. But the bear-raccoon-thing was startled enough to flail, giving Midas just enough time to grab Chewy and bolt. ### **The Return Journey** The trip home was significantly worse. For one, they had to find a new route, since retracing their steps would mean running into the very creature they had just annoyed. For another, Midas had failed to account for the local gang of scavengers, who had a keen eye for idiots carrying bags of food. "Alright," Midas said, panting as they ducked behind a rusted car. "New plan. Distraction." Chewy’s eyes flickered blue—reluctant agreement. A minute later, Midas burst from cover, waving his arms wildly. "Hey! I found a whole stash of food over there!" he lied, pointing in a direction that was definitely not where he had found food. The scavengers hesitated. Then Chewy, perched dramatically atop the car, let out a noise he had not made in years. "**Hug me! I'm your best friend!**" The scavengers, deeply unsettled by this sudden and unexpected proclamation, hesitated just long enough for Midas to bolt. By the time the scavengers realized they’d been tricked, Midas and Chewy were already halfway to safety. ### **Home, Worse for Wear** They stumbled back into their hideout—an abandoned bookstore, now home to dust, regret, and a growing collection of questionable decisions. Midas collapsed onto a makeshift cot, groaning. Chewy sat beside him, his eyes slowly cycling through colors. "That went well," Midas said. Chewy’s eyes turned purple. "Fine. It could have gone better." He pulled out their spoils: three cans of something edible, a bottle of maybe-not-poison, and a single slightly squashed loaf of bread. Chewy’s eyes flickered pink—relief. Midas sighed, breaking the loaf in half. "Alright, buddy. Tomorrow, we do better." Chewy’s eyes turned blue. They both knew he was lying.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece successfully captures the wry, dry humor characteristic of Terry Pratchett's style, with a post-apocalyptic setting that feels both bleak and absurdly comedic. The author demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt, delivering a complete narrative arc that follows the requested structure: deliberation, mission gone sideways, and return home worse for wear. The characterization is particularly effective. Midas emerges as a believably hapless protagonist whose optimism consistently outpaces his competence. The author cleverly uses Chewy's LED eye colors as a communication system, creating genuine character interaction despite the koala's mechanical nature. This device feels both practical for the post-tech world and endearingly absurd. The prose maintains consistent voice throughout, balancing deadpan observations with physical comedy. Lines like "Yesterday had involved a pack of feral dogs, an unexpectedly explosive propane tank, and a very short-lived attempt at diplomacy with a man named Gristle" exemplify the Pratchett-esque ability to pack absurd backstory into casual asides. The mission structure works well, with each obstacle escalating logically from the last. The missing bridge, the creature in the supermarket, and the scavenger encounter all feel organic to this world while serving the comedy. The author shows restraint in not over-explaining the apocalypse, letting details emerge naturally. However, some elements feel slightly underdeveloped. The emotional complexity remains surface-level, focusing primarily on the comedic partnership rather than exploring deeper themes of survival or companionship. The imagery, while functional, occasionally lacks the vivid specificity that would elevate it from competent to memorable. The dialogue system through Chewy's colors is creative but limits actual spoken interaction. When dialogue does occur ("NOT TODAY," "Hug me! I'm your best friend!"), it serves the comedy effectively but doesn't reveal much character depth. The ending maintains appropriate tone without false optimism - they're back where they started, marginally better supplied but no less likely to repeat their mistakes. This feels earned and consistent with the established dynamic. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 13 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 14 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 11 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 77.8
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Love in the Limelight** --- The bell above the door jangled violently as a man all but threw himself inside, slamming the door shut behind him. A gust of cold Cardiff air followed, along with the distant sound of shouting from the street. The man turned, breathless, and locked eyes with the woman behind the counter. “Apologies,” he said, flashing a grin so practiced it could have been patented. “Bit of a situation outside.” The woman, who had been stacking a neat pile of books beside the till, raised an unimpressed eyebrow. “You mean the herd of photographers currently pressed against my window?” He winced, glancing at the frosted glass where shadows loomed, camera flashes flickering like distant lightning. “Ah. Yes. That lot.” He turned back to her, leaning against the counter with an insouciance that suggested he was used to charming his way out of trouble. “Do you mind terribly if I loiter?” She considered him. Tall, dark-haired, devastatingly handsome in a way that suggested he knew it. His coat was expensive, his boots well-worn but stylish. And that smirk—oh, it could probably melt glaciers when deployed correctly. He was also, unfortunately, familiar. “You’re Rhys Llewellyn.” It wasn’t a question. He brightened. “So you *do* know me.” “I live in Cardiff, not under a rock.” She picked up a book and began flipping through it, as if he were of no more consequence than the dust motes swirling in the shop’s dim light. “You’re in all the tabloids.” He placed a hand over his heart, mock-wounded. “And yet you don’t seem thrilled to meet me. That’s unusual.” She hummed noncommittally. He watched her for a moment, clearly recalibrating. “May I ask your name, mysterious bookshop guardian?” She glanced up, debating whether to indulge him. “Elin,” she said finally. “Elin,” he repeated, as if tasting the name. “Lovely. Very... literary.” She snorted. “That’s a reach.” “What can I say? I’m a creative soul.” He gestured vaguely at the shop. “So, Elin, do you often provide sanctuary to beleaguered celebrities?” “Not intentionally.” “Ah, but fate has a way of throwing interesting people together, wouldn’t you say?” She gave him a flat look. “Do you ever stop talking?” His grin widened. “Rarely. It’s one of my more endearing traits.” She rolled her eyes and set her book down. “I’d argue the opposite.” Rhys let out a delighted laugh. “You’re *brilliant*—I think you might be the first person in a decade who hasn’t immediately tried to flatter me.” “Tragic,” she said dryly. “Devastating, really.” He leaned in slightly, lowering his voice conspiratorially. “I should warn you, though, your indifference only makes me more intrigued.” Elin felt her stomach do something annoying and fluttery but refused to let it show. “You mistake indifference for patience.” “Patience?” “Yes. I’m patiently waiting for you to leave so my shop isn’t plastered across tomorrow’s front pages.” He placed a hand dramatically over his chest. “You wound me.” “Do you even have the capacity to be wounded?” Rhys grinned, but there was a flicker of something in his expression—something fleeting and real. “More than you’d think.” She hesitated, caught off guard by the sudden shift. He recovered quickly, though, stepping away as if to browse the shelves. “So,” he said, tracing a finger along a row of spines. “What kind of bookshop do you run, Elin? Any particular specialty?” “Books,” she said dryly. He shot her an amused glance. “I walked into that one, didn’t I?” “You did.” He plucked a book from the shelf, flipping it open. “Do you have a favourite?” Elin hesitated. It was one of those questions that felt oddly intimate, as if the answer could reveal more about her than she was willing to share. “I have several.” “That’s a deflection.” “It’s the truth.” Rhys studied her, then looked back at the book in his hands. “You know,” he mused, “I think I like you.” “I’m sure you think that about most people.” He smirked. “Not really. I like being *liked* by most people. There’s a difference.” She tilted her head. “And yet, here I am, unimpressed.” “Exactly! It’s *refreshing*.” He set the book back on the shelf and leaned against it, watching her with a thoughtful expression. “You don’t seem particularly keen to impress *me* either.” “I’m not.” “See, I find that fascinating.” Elin sighed, pressing her fingers to her temples. “You must be *exhausting* to be around.” Rhys laughed, full and unguarded. “Oh, absolutely.” The bell above the door jingled again, and a teenage girl poked her head inside, eyes going comically wide at the sight of him. “Oh my *god*,” she breathed, before whipping out her phone. Rhys reacted instantly, stepping behind a tall bookshelf. “For the love of Shakespeare,” he muttered. Elin sighed and turned to the girl. “No photos.” The girl blinked. “But—” “No photos,” Elin repeated, firm. “You can either buy a book or leave.” The girl hesitated, then scowled and ducked back out. Rhys peeked around the shelf. “You’re a proper gatekeeper, aren’t you?” “I like my shop quiet.” “I like your shop too.” He hesitated, then said, almost shyly, “I like *you*.” Elin’s stomach did another annoying flip. She cleared her throat. “You don’t know me.” “Not yet.” His gaze was steady, no longer teasing. “But I’d like to.” She exhaled, aware of the heat rising up her neck. “You should go before they figure out where you are.” Rhys studied her for a beat longer, then nodded. “Alright.” He stepped closer, lowering his voice. “But tell me, Elin—if I came back, would you let me stay a little longer next time?” She met his gaze, pulse unreasonably fast. Then, because she was feeling reckless, she said, “Only if you actually buy a book.” His grin was slow and utterly devastating. “Deal.” And with that, he slipped out the door, vanishing into the Cardiff evening, leaving Elin standing in the quiet hush of her bookshop, heart pounding, entirely unsure of what had just happened. But she had a feeling it wasn’t over.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates a meet-cute scenario in a Welsh bookstore with two well-defined characters whose personalities emerge clearly through dialogue. Rhys is portrayed as a charming rogue with an evident need for validation, while Elin maintains her composure despite being inwardly affected by his presence. The dialogue carries the scene effectively, with each character having a distinct voice. Rhys speaks with practiced charm and theatrical flourishes ("For the love of Shakespeare"), while Elin responds with dry wit and measured responses. The banter feels natural and serves the dual purpose of revealing character while building romantic tension. The setting is efficiently established with minimal but effective details - the frosted glass windows, the bell above the door, the shadows of photographers outside. The Welsh setting is acknowledged through location (Cardiff) and the character's Welsh name (Rhys Llewellyn), though it could have been more deeply integrated. The romantic chemistry builds believably through their verbal sparring. Rhys's genuine interest is sparked by Elin's resistance to his charm, while her internal reactions (the "annoying fluttery" feeling) contrast nicely with her outward composure. The scene maintains appropriate tension without rushing toward resolution. However, the piece has some weaknesses. The character dynamics, while well-executed, follow fairly predictable romance tropes. The "immune to charm" setup is familiar territory. Some moments feel slightly overwrought, particularly Rhys's more theatrical declarations. The ending, while satisfying, resolves perhaps too neatly with the book-buying compromise. The prose is generally clean and readable, though occasionally slips into telling rather than showing, particularly regarding Elin's internal state. The pacing works well, building from the dramatic entrance to the quieter, more intimate moments of connection. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 14 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 4 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 15
